title: Offices of Real Estate Agents and Brokers

labor_share.method: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 531210 ($30,836,359,913) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 531210 ($180,013,344,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

occupations.title: Property, Real Estate, and Community Association Managers
Janitors and Cleaners, Except Maids and Housekeeping Cleaners
Secretaries and Administrative Assistants, Except Legal, Medical, and Executive
First-Line Supervisors of Mechanics, Installers, and Repairers

n_band.derivation: margin 0.0396 (margins.json[53]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Real Estate (Operations & Services) EBITDA/Sales 3.96% (agency/brokerage/property-management economics, dominant in firm counts)); revenue/employee $432,174 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 531210); class <5: 140,557 firms, avg revenue/firm $449,562 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $17,803 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 6,638 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,862,814 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $113,367 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 2,095 firms, avg revenue/firm $6,465,620 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $256,039 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 1,204 firms, avg revenue/firm $16,484,213 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $652,775 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 259 firms, avg revenue/firm $48,286,429 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,912,143 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 139 firms, avg revenue/firm $373,541,367 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $14,792,238 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 259
n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Real Estate (Operations & Services) EBITDA/Sales 3.96% (agency/brokerage/property-management economics, dominant in firm counts)

breakdown_by_role.role: Maintenance and Repair Workers (49-9071) - 3-digit proxy contamination
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Physical building operations from sibling codes (lessors/property managers), largely absent from actual 531210 brokerage offices. Not automatable either way. Contamination flagged per prompt guidance.

breakdown_by_role.role: Property, Real Estate, and Community Association Managers (11-9141) - largely proxy contamination
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Mostly lessor/property-management contamination, not brokerage staff. Where present, the role is relationship/judgment work with a modest reporting/scheduling/communication sliver AI handles.

breakdown_by_role.role: Real Estate Sales Agents, W-2 slice (41-9022)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: AI already does much of the task list (listing prep, CMA/pricing drafts, marketing copy, scheduling, disclosure paperwork) but the state license required to collect a commission and the trust/negotiation relationship are not automatable. Only the W-2 salaried slice is in the wage bill; the vast majority of agents are 1099 and outside labor_share entirely.

breakdown_by_role.role: Counter and Rental Clerks (41-2021) - largely proxy contamination
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Rental-desk contamination from lessors; where present, application processing, booking and inquiry handling are highly automatable, with an in-person service remainder.
Listing administration, file compliance, data entry - the clean AI surface in a brokerage office.
Recruiting funnels, production reporting and office admin automate; managing-broker compliance supervision is statutory (a licensed broker must supervise agents) and persists.
Physical work from sibling codes; not brokerage staff, not automatable.
Scheduling, correspondence, document assembly - same clean surface as clerks.
Commission disbursement accounting, trust-account reconciliation and AP/AR automate substantially; escrow/trust compliance review persists.

breakdown_by_role.role: First-Line Supervisors of Mechanics (49-1011) - proxy contamination
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Building-trades supervision from sibling codes; not brokerage staff, not automatable.

breakdown_by_role.role: Remainder / unlisted tail (marketing, IT, transaction coordination, leasing staff, real estate brokers 41-9021)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: At 531210 proper this tail is mostly marketing/content (heavily automatable), transaction coordinators (heavily automatable) and licensed brokers/IT/support (partial or not). Blended 0.30; the tail is the single largest driver of the estimate and inherits the LOW-quality proxy problem.

pi_dist.rationale: The operator (brokerage) does not own the client or the savings. The agent's book is personal and fully portable; upstream, Zillow/Realtor.com/Homes.com and the MLSs control search traffic and sell leads back to agents, so AI improvements in discovery/matching accrue to portals, not brokerages. The brokerage's retained 'company dollar' is thin (Damodaran ops-services margin 3.96% on receipts) and split competition is the documented deflation mechanism: any AI-driven agent productivity gain strengthens top agents' bargaining power and gets competed away as higher splits (100%-split and cap models like eXp already leave the brokerage a fee, not a margin). The one genuine retention channel: commission pricing is market-set, not cost-based, so savings on the small W-2 admin base are not contractually passed to consumers - hence 0.25 rather than lower. Post-settlement commission rates have so far held (buyer side ~2.82% Feb 2026 vs ~2.6% pre-settlement per Clever/AccountTECH tracking), so consumer-side clawback is not yet realized.

pi_moat.rationale: The statutory moat is real but narrow: state license law requires a licensed agent/broker to collect a commission, so full generic-AI bypass of licensed intermediation is legally blocked. Everything else is weak: an acquirer of a brokerage owns almost nothing durable - agents can leave next day with their books, brands are often franchised (separate royalty stream), offices are increasingly virtual, and the bypass path (flat-fee MLS listing + AI-drafted paperwork + transaction attorney) is legal and cheapening. MLS/portal gatekeeping protects the industry's data position but is owned by MLSs/portals, not by an acquirable brokerage.

t50_years.derivation: current_adoption_pct: NAR's 2025 Technology Survey has 68% of Realtors using AI, but only 17% report significant business impact and 33% moderate - material firm-level deployment in the automatable functions (marketing content, transaction coordination, back-office) is estimated at ~30% of brokerage firms today. Historical analog: eSignature - a comparable workflow technology - is now at 79% member use and went from niche (post-2005 ESIGN-era tools) to majority use in roughly 8-10 years; AI rides free consumer tools (ChatGPT 58% of members) with no hardware or regulatory gate, and this industry historically adopts consumer-facing tech faster than back-office tech. From ~30% material today at the observed momentum (68% casual use converting to material use as tools embed in MLS/CRM/TC platforms): ~2.5 years to 50% material adoption.

current_adoption_pct.source: NAR 2025 REALTORS Technology Survey (random sample of 49,233 active Realtors, fielded July 2025); HousingWire coverage
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: AI adoption reached 68% of agents; most common tool ChatGPT (58%); but only 17% report AI having a significant positive impact and 33% moderately positive, while 46% see no noticeable difference. Material = firm-level paid deployment of AI in marketing/listing content, transaction coordination or back-office workflows (not casual chatbot use); estimated at ~30% of firms = roughly the significant+half-of-moderate impact share.

historical_analogs.value: eSignature in real estate: from early tools (post-2000 ESIGN Act, DocuSign founded 2003) to majority use took roughly 8-12 years, crossing ~50% of members in the mid-2010s; now 79% (2025 NAR survey). Social media for business use followed a similar sub-decade curve to 75%. Real estate adopts consumer-grade workflow tech fast; back-office/MLS tech slower.
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: eSignature (79%) and social media (75%) remain the most widely used tech tools among Realtors.

owners_60plus_pct.source: NAR 2025 Member Profile (via RealEstateNews/Houston Agent coverage of the report)
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: Median member age 57, up from 55 in 2023; 'the share of agents who are 60 or older has jumped from 35% to 44%'; only 11% under 40. Members-60+ used as the owner proxy; broker-owners skew older than the membership, so 0.44 is conservative for owners.

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: Aging membership and a thinning under-40 pipeline are documented (NAR 2025 Member Profile), but no primary source documents a succession shortage of brokerage OWNERS specifically (unsold firms, no-heir closures); RTC commentary notes weaker valuations reflect performance, not absent buyers. Flag left false.

active_consolidators.source: Deal coverage 2024-2026: RealEstateNews/HousingWire (Compass-@properties/Christie's, Compass-Anywhere), Peerage Realty (PitchBook profile; Premier Sotheby's expansion), The Agency, William Raveis
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: Consolidation is heavy but strategic-led, not PE-platform-led: Compass (public) acquired Christie's/@properties ($444M, closed Jan 2025) and signed the Anywhere merger (Sep 2025, ~$10B EV); PE-backed platforms doing brokerage add-ons in the last 24 months are roughly three - Peerage Realty Partners (serial Sotheby's-affiliate add-ons), The Agency (investor-backed, 27 new offices 2025, occasional acquisitions), and family/investor-backed William Raveis (e.g., Jordan Real Estate, Nantucket). Search found no HVAC-style PE roll-up cohort in residential brokerage.

buy_mult.source: CT Acquisitions real estate brokerage valuation guide; RTC Consulting market commentary; DealStream rules of thumb
buy_mult.figure_quoted: 'Real estate brokerage business valuation in 2026: 0.5-1.5x GCI or 3-5x EBITDA'; real estate services broadly 3-8x with small/seasonal operations at the lower end; RTC notes multiples have held but valuations fell on weaker earnings, with more deferred consideration. Used 3.5x = lower-middle of the 3-5x small-firm range, on EBITDA after market-rate owner compensation (RTC-style company-dollar-based EBITDA, not GCI).

exit_mult.source: Compass-Anywhere merger filings/coverage; CT Acquisitions/RTC range top end for scaled diversified platforms
exit_mult.figure_quoted: Compass-Anywhere all-stock merger at ~$10B combined enterprise value (announced Sep 22, 2025) with >$225M run-rate synergies - implying high-single-digit EV/EBITDA for a scaled platform whose multiple is propped by franchise royalty and title/escrow streams; pure-brokerage services range tops out near 8x ('3x to 8x EBITDA... stable, well-diversified brokerage... at the higher end'). Used 7.0x for a $10M+ EBITDA diversified platform.

pricing_structure.value: Essentially 100% of brokerage revenue is percentage-of-transaction-value commission (avg total ~5.7% as of Feb 2026: ~2.88% listing side, ~2.82% buyer side), of which 70-90%+ passes through to 1099 agents under contractual splits; the brokerage retains thin 'company dollar' plus per-transaction/desk fees (100%-split and cap models) and, at franchisors, royalty fees. No hourly, retainer, or cost-plus billing of consequence; post-settlement (Aug 2024) buyer-agent compensation is negotiated separately but rates have rebounded to roughly pre-settlement levels.
pricing_structure.source: Clever 2026 commission survey; AccountTECH 224,000-transaction study; Redfin commission tracking
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: 'The average total commission is about 5.70 percent as of February 2026, split into a 2.88 percent listing-side fee and a 2.82 percent buyer's-side fee'; AccountTECH: buyer-agent commission 2.55% in January 2025, 'identical to the rate from one year prior' across 224,000+ transactions at ~1,300 brokerage offices; compression so far only at the $1M+ tier (2.30% Q1 2024 to 2.17% Q1 2025).

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: V_raw = 0.0424 implies ~4.24pp of revenue (receipts basis) at full realization. A = 4.0 < 5 and uplift >= 3pp, so the inconsistency trigger (uplift < 3pp with A >= 5) does not fire. Note the receipts basis understates the operator-relevant effect: on retained company dollar the same W-2 savings are a much larger share of the brokerage's own P&L - but per the formulas, V is computed on receipts.

terminal_value.justification: Two failure modes, kept separate. (1) AI cutting brokerage office costs: real but small - the W-2 base is only 18.26% of receipts and much of the true cost line (agent splits) is a pass-through AI cannot harvest. (2) AI plus fee compression shrinking the commission pool: the post-settlement evidence says NOT YET - buyer-agent rates dipped after Aug 2024, then recovered to ~2.82% by Feb 2026 (Redfin/AccountTECH/Clever), with compression only at the $1M+ tier. Consumer trust in the largest transaction of their lives plus the statutory license requirement is holding the full-commission model for now. But the structural direction is one-way: buyer-agent decoupling is in place, portals and AI make discovery/valuation self-serve, the bypass wrapper (flat-fee MLS + AI paperwork + attorney) gets cheaper every year, and any eventual compression comes straight out of the commission pool that is this industry's entire revenue. Demand for licensed intermediation persists but the paid-value-per-transaction is under secular pressure: PRESSURED, not MELTING - the rate evidence does not yet show the pool shrinking - and not DURABLE, because the operator's product is exactly the layer AI plus decoupling squeezes.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: Confirmed: V was built from the OEWS role mix (with contamination flagged) and task-level analysis of brokerage functions; A was derived from the NAR 2025 Technology Survey and the eSignature diffusion analog. Neither used deal activity. Compass-Anywhere, Compass-Christie's/@properties, Peerage et al. informed only active_consolidators (B) and the multiples (M).
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Billing is ~100% percentage-of-value commission with 70-90%+ contractually passed through to 1099 agents. AI efficiency leaks out of the operator through two channels: (a) split competition - agent productivity gains raise top-producer bargaining power and are competed away as richer splits/cap models, and (b) prospective consumer-side compression post-decoupling (not yet realized in rates: buyer side back to ~2.82%). There is no cost-plus/audited pass-through, so savings on the small W-2 admin base are retainable - which is why pi_dist is 0.25 rather than 0.1. pi_moat 0.35 carries the statutory-license floor against full AI bypass, offset by zero agent lock-in and portal/MLS gatekeeping of lead flow. Together C = 0.875, the binding constraint in this score.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A ranges 2.5-6.667; S ranges ~2.51-3.06 (S scales as (A/4.0)^0.2 around 2.761)

top_uncertain_inputs.input: ai_replaceable_share (tail fraction; role_mix is LOW-quality 3-digit proxy)

top_uncertain_inputs.plausible_range: 5.0-9.0 (scaled-platform comps are franchise/title-inflated; pure brokerage lower)

reviewer_note: STRUCTURAL MIS-FIT (per prompt trap list, confronted head-on): the operator's biggest cost line - agent commission splits - is 1099 pass-through, not W-2 labor, so an AI-labor thesis cannot harvest it; labor_share 0.1826 is among the lowest in the screen and V = 1.696 correctly reflects that only a thin W-2 admin/marketing wrapper is automatable on a receipts basis. ROLE-MIX CONTAMINATION: role_mix is the 3-digit 531 proxy (quality LOW) - maintenance workers, janitors, mechanics supervisors, most property managers and rental clerks (~35-37% of the proxy wage bill) are lessor/property-manager staff, not brokerage staff; their fractions were set at/near zero and the true brokerage V structure lives mostly in the 0.3782 unlisted tail (fraction 0.30), making ai_replaceable_share LOW quality. heterogeneous = false (not a catch-all; residential brokerage dominates 531210), but the proxy contamination is recorded here instead. DATASET CONFLICTS: none material; the 3.96% Damodaran margin on receipts is consistent with pass-through economics (never compare GCI-basis and company-dollar-basis multiples - buy_mult/exit_mult here are EBITDA-basis). CONSOLIDATOR COUNT: consolidation is heavy but strategic-led (Compass, HomeServices); genuinely PE-backed add-on platforms are ~3 (ESTIMATE) - if one instead counted all active strategic consolidators (~8), CFD rises to 0.477 and B falls to ~6.45, S to ~2.74 (minor). M = 4.0 warrants skepticism: the 3.5x->7.0x spread is real in quoted ranges, but the 7x end embeds franchise-royalty and title/escrow streams a brokerage roll-up would have to build, and n_band = 259 with agent-portability risk makes the arbitrage hard to execute; the formula result stands per rule 1. FORMULA VS INTUITION: S = 2.761 feels a touch generous for a business where the acquired asset can walk out the door - the weakness is correctly concentrated in V (1.70) and C (0.875), and B/M carry the score; flagged for the reviewer. Post-settlement commission structure is in live flux; pricing inputs are 2025-2026 vintage and should be re-run if rates start compressing.
